content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement
(916) 322-5642 322-5660 322-5901 322-6441

December 28, 1983

Lynn L. Crandell, Director
East Kern Hospital District
P.O. Box 2313
California City, CA 93505

Re: Your Request for Advice, Our
Advice No. A-83-256

Dear Mrs. Crandell:

I am in receipt of your letter of November 10, 1983,
requesting advice concerning a situation we previously discussed
by phone. From our discussion and your letter, my understanding
of the situation is as follows.

You are a Director for the East Kern Hospital District
(hereinafter the "District"). In addition, you are a Call-Paid
Firefighter for California City (hereinafter the "City"), and
you receive $5.00 for every call that you respond to. Your
husband is Fire Chief for the City. The District and the
City are distinct, unaffiliated public entities.

You stated that the District is currently subsidizing a
private ambulance company to keep an ambulance in the City. The
Board for the District is exploring alternatives to this
situation to reduce the cost to the District. Since the City
Fire Department has an ambulance, it has been suggested that the
District and the City could jointly operate an ambulance
service. The Board appointed a committee including yourself to
meet with the City Manager to discuss this option. At this
stage, the plans are very tentative, and, in any event, a final
decision on the matter would have to be made by the City Council
and the Board acting as a whole.

You asked whether your participation in this matter
constitutes a conflict of interest under the Political Reform
Act.

Lynn L. Crandell, Director
December 28, 1983
Page 2


Government Code Section 87101/ of the Act provides:

NO public official at any level of state or local
government shall make, participate in making or in any
way attempt to use his official position to influence a
governmental decision in which he knows or has reason
to know he has a financial interest.

An official has a financial interest in a decision if it is
reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material
financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public
generally...1/

(c) Any source of income, other than loans by a
commercial lending institution in the regular course of
business on terms available to the public without
regard to official status, aggregating two hundred
fifty dollars ($250) or more in value provided to,
received by or promised to the public official within
12 months prior to the time when the decision is made....

Section 87103.

Thus, in your capacity as a Director for the District, you
should not participate in any decisions which will foreseeably
have a material financial effect on any source of income to you
of $250 or more.

Income is defined in the Act to include the official's
community property interest in her spouse's income, but the
definition excludes salary from a local government agency,
Section 82030. Accordingly, your husband's salary from the City
is not "income" within the meaning of the AGC, and his employ-
ment cannot be a basis for your disqualification on District
matters which might affect the City. The same analysis would
apply to any monies you receive from the City for being a
Call-Paid Firefighter.

In any event, the fact is that the City and not the District
determines the terms of your husband's compensation as Fire
Chief and the terms of your compensation as a Firefighter. Thus
it does not appear that the District is, or will be, making or
participating in any governmental decisions which could have a
financial effect within the meaning of Section 87103 upon your
or your spouse. This includes the District's negotiations with

1/ All statutory references are to the California
Government Code.

Lynn L. Crandell, Director
December 28, 1983
Page 3


tne City concerning a joint ambulance service. Even if there
could be financial effects upon you from decisions concerning
the ambulance service, at this point, such effects would be
extremely speculative and remote and could not be the basis for
disqualification.

In summary, you do not need to disqualify yourself under the
Political Reform Act from acting either as a part of the
planning committee for the Board or as a part of the Board on
matters concerning a joint venture with the City to provide a
full-time ambulance service in the City.

If you have additional questions or would like to discuss
this further, please feel free to contact me at (916) 322-6444.

Sincerely,

Diane Maura Fishburn
Counsel
Legal Division

DMS:pln

P.O. Box 2313
California City Ca 93505
November 10, 1983

Fair Political Practices Commission
1100 K Street
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, Ca 95804

Commissioners:

On November 07, 1983, I talked with attorney Diane Fishburn,
trying to figure out if I am in Conflict of Interest. I would
like written advice as to whether or not I am in Conflict.

I am a Director for the East Kern Hospital District here in
California City. I am also a Call-Paid Firefighter, and my
husband is the Fire Chief for the City of California City. The
Hospital District is not affiliated with the City. I was told
I would be in Conflict for the following situation.

The District is currently subsidizing a private ambulance
company to keep an ambulance in California City. The board now
feels the amount is to high and we are looking into other options
that we could go. The City Fire Department has their own ambulance
which we use in critical emergencies when the private ambulance
is not available. It was brought up at one of our meetings to have
a committee meet with the City Manager to discuss the possibilities
of a joint venture between the City and the District of running an
ambulance full time. I was appointed to that committee. When it
came time for the meeting, the City Manager said he had gotten a
phone call from the East Kern Hospital District's attorney (who is
also the City's attorney) that I would be in Conflict if I attended
the meeting as I was also a Call-Paid Firefighter for the City. I
get $5.00 only when we are paged out for a call that I respond to.

As far as this situation was concerned, the committee was only
to relay the boards feelings to the City Manager who in turn would
have to refer the proposal to the City Council. Then a decision
would have to be made by the City Council. Until then nothing else
can be accomplished until the City Council and the District decide
on this joint venture.

I am only a Director for the Hospital District and have no
control over what the City will say or do, if and when they take
over this service full time.

-1-

I don't know how I can be in conflict when no one knows what
is going to take place. Everything is in talking stages only. I
would like to give a situation of what could happen and then tell
me if it did how I would stand.

If the Fire Department took over the service full time and
had a full time crew of 2 people (not including myself) and on the
two days they were off, there would be a stand-by rouster where I
could be put on stand-by and if we got an ambulance run and I was
paged out, that would be the only time we would get paid. The
private ambulance company stated they receive approximately 4-7
ambulance calls per month in the district area. So you can see
with only five small number of calls, there is no way I could see
making a substantial financial gain over this venture as the odds
of all those calls happening on my shift is impossible.

I would like also the following questions answered if possible.

1. What would be considered to be a substantial financial
gain to me in this situation, not knowing what we will
be paid if the city gets into this service?

2. If I were to discuss or vote on the City and District
taking this venture, would I be in Conflict?

3. With my husband being the Fire Chief, could they say,
that could be in Conflict?

Any help you can give me in this matter would be greatly appreciated. I do hope you can understand what I am trying to say and
wanting answers for, so I know where I stand as far as being in
conflict.

Thank you in advance for your time and cooperation.

Sincerely,

[signature]
(Mrs.) Lynn L. Crandell
Director,
East Kern Hospital District

-2-

sections.question: You asked whether your participation in this matter constitutes a conflict of interest under the Political Reform Act.
sections.conclusion: In summary, you do not need to disqualify yourself under the Political Reform Act from acting either as a part of the planning committee for the Board or as a part of the Board on matters concerning a Joint venture with the City to provide a full-time ambulance service in the City.
sections.question_synthetic: Whether a Hospital District Director who is also a Call-Paid Firefighter for the City (whose husband is the Fire Chief) may participate in a committee and board decisions regarding a proposed joint ambulance service between the District and City without violating conflict of interest laws.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The requestor does not have a disqualifying financial interest because salary from local government agencies is excluded from the definition of income under the Act, and any potential financial effects from the ambulance service decision are too speculative and remote to trigger disqualification.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document includes both the incoming request letter (November 10, 1983) and the FPPC response letter (December 28, 1983). The response letter from Counsel Diane Maura Fishburn contains clear question and conclusion sections. Requestor asked three numbered follow-up questions in original request, but FPPC addressed the main question in comprehensive analysis.

QUESTION: You asked whether your participation in this matter constitutes a conflict of interest under the Political Reform Act.

CONCLUSION: In summary, you do not need to disqualify yourself under the Political Reform Act from acting either as a part of the planning committee for the Board or as a part of the Board on matters concerning a Joint venture with the City to provide a full-time ambulance service in the City.

embedding.summary: FPPC advises a Hospital District Director with City firefighting income and a spouse who is a City Fire Chief that she may participate in District decisions regarding a joint ambulance service with the City, as local government salaries are excluded from conflict of interest analysis and any financial effects are too speculative.
